text:
```json
[
  "Management of main memory is critical for system performance.",
  "System performance depends on the amount of available memory and its optimization during job processing.",
  "This chapter introduces the memory manager (RAM) and core memory (primary storage).",
  "Four types of early memory allocation schemes are introduced: Single-user systems, Fixed partitions, Dynamic partitions, and Relocatable dynamic partitions.",
  "These early memory management schemes are important to study because they introduced fundamental concepts, even though they are seldom used by today’s operating systems.",
  "The Single-User Contiguous Scheme was commercially available in the 1940s and 1950s.",
  "In the Single-User Contiguous Scheme, each program was loaded entirely into memory and allocated as much contiguous space as it needed.",
  "If a program was too large for available memory in the Single-User Contiguous Scheme, it could not be executed.",
  "Early computers, despite being physically large, had very little memory.",
  "To run a program that doesn't fit, main memory must be increased, or the program must be modified, for example, by using methods that allow program segments to be overlaid.",
  "Overlays allow program segments to take turns occupying the same memory locations.",
  "The Single-User Contiguous Scheme is a non-networked environment where each user accesses all available main memory, and jobs are processed sequentially.",
  "The Memory Manager in a Single-User Contiguous Scheme evaluates incoming jobs for size, loads them if they fit, or rejects them.",
  "The Memory Manager monitors occupied memory and indicates when the entire main memory space is available after a resident job ends.",
  "A major problem with the Single-User Contiguous Scheme is its lack of support for multiprogramming.",
  "The Single-User Contiguous Scheme was unacceptable for the business community because it was not cost-effective to use expensive equipment for only one person at a time.",
  "The Memory Manager in a Single-User Contiguous Scheme requires only a base address register and an accumulator to track program size.",
  "Once loaded, a program remains in memory until its execution is complete in the Single-User Contiguous Scheme.",
  "Fixed Partitions were the first attempt to implement multiprogramming.",
  "In Fixed Partitions, main memory is divided into static partitions, with one partition designated for each job.",
  "The size of each fixed partition was set when the system powered on and could only be reconfigured when the system shut down.",
  "Fixed Partitions introduced protection of a job's memory space, preventing other jobs from entering its boundaries.",
  "Fixed Partitions are more flexible than the Single-User scheme because they allow several programs to be in memory simultaneously.",
  "Fixed Partitions still require the entire program to be stored contiguously and remain in memory from beginning to end of execution.",
  "The OS's Memory Manager for Fixed Partitions keeps a table detailing each partition's size, address, access restrictions, and current status (free or busy).",
  "If fixed partition sizes are too small, larger jobs may be rejected or experience longer turnaround times.",
  "If fixed partition sizes are too big, memory is wasted because unused space within a partition cannot be allocated to another job.",
  "Internal fragmentation is a major drawback of the fixed partition scheme, caused by partial usage of partitions and the creation of unused spaces within them.",
  "In Dynamic Partitions, available memory is kept in contiguous blocks, but jobs are given only as much memory as they request when loaded for processing.",
  "A dynamic partition scheme fully utilizes memory when the first jobs are loaded.",
  "Subsequent memory allocation in dynamic partitions creates fragments of free memory between allocated blocks, known as external fragmentation.",
  "External fragmentation leads to wasted memory.",
  "For both fixed and dynamic memory allocation schemes, the OS must maintain lists of free and busy memory locations.",
  "Free partitions can be allocated using either First-fit or Best-fit memory allocation methods.",
  "First-fit memory allocation selects the first partition that fits the job's requirements.",
  "Best-fit memory allocation selects the smallest partition that fits the job's requirements, aiming for the least wasted space.",
  "The Best-fit allocation method keeps free/busy lists ordered by size (smallest to largest) to make the best use of memory space.",
  "The First-fit method keeps free/busy lists organized by memory locations (low-order to high-order), making allocation faster.",
  "The First-fit algorithm searches for the first memory block large enough to fit a job and places the job there.",
  "If no block is found by the First-fit algorithm, the job is placed into a waiting queue.",
  "The Best-fit algorithm's goal is to find the smallest memory block into which a job will fit, requiring a search of the entire memory table.",
  "Hypothetical allocation schemes include Next-fit, which starts searching from the last allocated block, and Worst-fit, which allocates the largest free block.",
  "Memory space must eventually be released or deallocated.",
  "In a fixed-partition system, deallocation involves the Memory Manager resetting the status of a completed job's memory block to 'free'.",
  "In a dynamic-partition system, a more complex deallocation algorithm is used to combine free areas of memory.",
  "Deallocation in dynamic partitions handles three cases: when the block is adjacent to another free block, when it is between two free blocks, or when it is isolated.",
  "If a block to be deallocated is adjacent to another free block, the list is updated to reflect a new, larger free block combining the two.",
  "If a block to be deallocated is between two free blocks, the sizes of all three are combined, and the entry for the middle block is marked as null.",
  "If a block to be deallocated is isolated, the system searches for a null entry in the table, then enters the job's beginning memory location, size, and changes the status to 'free'.",
  "Relocatable Dynamic Partitions were developed to resolve fragmentation issues and utilize small, leftover memory slivers.",
  "In Relocatable Dynamic Partitions, the Memory Manager relocates programs to compact all empty blocks into one large block of memory.",
  "Compaction reclaims fragmented sections of memory space by making all programs in memory contiguous.",
  "During compaction, every address and reference within each program must be adjusted to account for the program's new location in memory.",
  "After relocation and compaction, both the free list and the busy list are updated to reflect new memory configurations.",
  "Special-purpose registers, including a bounds register and a relocation register, are used for relocation.",
  "A bounds register stores the highest or lowest memory location accessible by a program to ensure memory protection.",
  "A relocation register contains the value that must be added to each address referenced in a program to access correct memory addresses after relocation.",
  "Compacting and relocating optimize memory use and improve throughput, but they incur more overhead than previous schemes.",
  "The timing of compaction is a crucial factor, with options including performing it when a certain percentage of memory is busy, only when jobs are waiting, or after a prescribed amount of time.",
  "The best choice for compaction timing is decided by the OS designer to optimize processing time and memory use while keeping overhead low.",
  "Common requirements of the four early memory management techniques include loading the entire program into memory, contiguous storage, and memory residency until the job is completed.",
  "These early techniques placed severe restrictions on job size.",
  "They were sufficient for the first three generations of computers, which processed jobs in batch mode with turnaround times measured in hours.",
  "Modern memory management trends, emerging in the late 1960s and early 1970s, involve programs not being stored in contiguous memory and not all segments residing in memory during job execution."
]
```
json_data: Management of main memory is critical for system performance.
System performance depends on the amount of available memory and its optimization during job processing.
This chapter introduces the memory manager (RAM) and core memory (primary storage).
Four types of early memory allocation schemes are introduced: Single-user systems, Fixed partitions, Dynamic partitions, and Relocatable dynamic partitions.
These early memory management schemes are important to study because they introduced fundamental concepts, even though they are seldom used by today’s operating systems.
The Single-User Contiguous Scheme was commercially available in the 1940s and 1950s.
In the Single-User Contiguous Scheme, each program was loaded entirely into memory and allocated as much contiguous space as it needed.
If a program was too large for available memory in the Single-User Contiguous Scheme, it could not be executed.
Early computers, despite being physically large, had very little memory.
To run a program that doesn't fit, main memory must be increased, or the program must be modified, for example, by using methods that allow program segments to be overlaid.
Overlays allow program segments to take turns occupying the same memory locations.
The Single-User Contiguous Scheme is a non-networked environment where each user accesses all available main memory, and jobs are processed sequentially.
The Memory Manager in a Single-User Contiguous Scheme evaluates incoming jobs for size, loads them if they fit, or rejects them.
The Memory Manager monitors occupied memory and indicates when the entire main memory space is available after a resident job ends.
A major problem with the Single-User Contiguous Scheme is its lack of support for multiprogramming.
The Single-User Contiguous Scheme was unacceptable for the business community because it was not cost-effective to use expensive equipment for only one person at a time.
The Memory Manager in a Single-User Contiguous Scheme requires only a base address register and an accumulator to track program size.
Once loaded, a program remains in memory until its execution is complete in the Single-User Contiguous Scheme.
Fixed Partitions were the first attempt to implement multiprogramming.
In Fixed Partitions, main memory is divided into static partitions, with one partition designated for each job.
The size of each fixed partition was set when the system powered on and could only be reconfigured when the system shut down.
Fixed Partitions introduced protection of a job's memory space, preventing other jobs from entering its boundaries.
Fixed Partitions are more flexible than the Single-User scheme because they allow several programs to be in memory simultaneously.
Fixed Partitions still require the entire program to be stored contiguously and remain in memory from beginning to end of execution.
The OS's Memory Manager for Fixed Partitions keeps a table detailing each partition's size, address, access restrictions, and current status (free or busy).
If fixed partition sizes are too small, larger jobs may be rejected or experience longer turnaround times.
If fixed partition sizes are too big, memory is wasted because unused space within a partition cannot be allocated to another job.
Internal fragmentation is a major drawback of the fixed partition scheme, caused by partial usage of partitions and the creation of unused spaces within them.
In Dynamic Partitions, available memory is kept in contiguous blocks, but jobs are given only as much memory as they request when loaded for processing.
A dynamic partition scheme fully utilizes memory when the first jobs are loaded.
Subsequent memory allocation in dynamic partitions creates fragments of free memory between allocated blocks, known as external fragmentation.
External fragmentation leads to wasted memory.
For both fixed and dynamic memory allocation schemes, the OS must maintain lists of free and busy memory locations.
Free partitions can be allocated using either First-fit or Best-fit memory allocation methods.
First-fit memory allocation selects the first partition that fits the job's requirements.
Best-fit memory allocation selects the smallest partition that fits the job's requirements, aiming for the least wasted space.
The Best-fit allocation method keeps free/busy lists ordered by size (smallest to largest) to make the best use of memory space.
The First-fit method keeps free/busy lists organized by memory locations (low-order to high-order), making allocation faster.
The First-fit algorithm searches for the first memory block large enough to fit a job and places the job there.
If no block is found by the First-fit algorithm, the job is placed into a waiting queue.
The Best-fit algorithm's goal is to find the smallest memory block into which a job will fit, requiring a search of the entire memory table.
Hypothetical allocation schemes include Next-fit, which starts searching from the last allocated block, and Worst-fit, which allocates the largest free block.
Memory space must eventually be released or deallocated.
In a fixed-partition system, deallocation involves the Memory Manager resetting the status of a completed job's memory block to 'free'.
In a dynamic-partition system, a more complex deallocation algorithm is used to combine free areas of memory.
Deallocation in dynamic partitions handles three cases: when the block is adjacent to another free block, when it is between two free blocks, or when it is isolated.
If a block to be deallocated is adjacent to another free block, the list is updated to reflect a new, larger free block combining the two.
If a block to be deallocated is between two free blocks, the sizes of all three are combined, and the entry for the middle block is marked as null.
If a block to be deallocated is isolated, the system searches for a null entry in the table, then enters the job's beginning memory location, size, and changes the status to 'free'.
Relocatable Dynamic Partitions were developed to resolve fragmentation issues and utilize small, leftover memory slivers.
In Relocatable Dynamic Partitions, the Memory Manager relocates programs to compact all empty blocks into one large block of memory.
Compaction reclaims fragmented sections of memory space by making all programs in memory contiguous.
During compaction, every address and reference within each program must be adjusted to account for the program's new location in memory.
After relocation and compaction, both the free list and the busy list are updated to reflect new memory configurations.
Special-purpose registers, including a bounds register and a relocation register, are used for relocation.
A bounds register stores the highest or lowest memory location accessible by a program to ensure memory protection.
A relocation register contains the value that must be added to each address referenced in a program to access correct memory addresses after relocation.
Compacting and relocating optimize memory use and improve throughput, but they incur more overhead than previous schemes.
The timing of compaction is a crucial factor, with options including performing it when a certain percentage of memory is busy, only when jobs are waiting, or after a prescribed amount of time.
The best choice for compaction timing is decided by the OS designer to optimize processing time and memory use while keeping overhead low.
Common requirements of the four early memory management techniques include loading the entire program into memory, contiguous storage, and memory residency until the job is completed.
These early techniques placed severe restrictions on job size.
They were sufficient for the first three generations of computers, which processed jobs in batch mode with turnaround times measured in hours.
Modern memory management trends, emerging in the late 1960s and early 1970s, involve programs not being stored in contiguous memory and not all segments residing in memory during job execution.
